diffs.replacement:
# Nextcloud FAQ Bot: Architecture & Process Guide This document outlines the optional backend architecture for integrating **Nextcloud** as the central storage and management system for the Wohnpro Guide. ## 1. Project Goal The primary goal of this integration is to automate the knowledge ingestion process for the AI-based FAQ Bot. Instead of manually typing data into the bot or managing complex databases, this system allows administrators to simply drag and drop standard business documents (PDFs, Word Docs) into a cloud folder. The system automatically transforms these documents into a format the bot can understand (plain text), ensuring the FAQ bot is always up-to-date with the latest company policies, price lists, and manuals. --- ## 2. Nextcloud Configuration To facilitate this automation, the Nextcloud instance is configured to act as both the **Trigger** (notifying the system of changes) and the **Storage** (holding both original and processed files). ### A. Folder Structure To prevent infinite processing loops and maintain organization, we use a strictly separated folder structure: **Root Folder:** `FAQ-Bot-Project` * **📂 01-Upload-Here (Source)** * **Purpose:** This is the "Human Zone." * **Action:** Users upload, update, or delete PDF/DOCX files here. * **Webhook:** This folder is monitored by the system. * **📂 02-Bot-Memory (Destination)** * **Purpose:** This is the "Machine Zone." * **Action:** The Node.js application saves the extracted `.txt` files here. * **Bot Access:** The FAQ Bot reads strictly from this folder to answer questions. * **📂 99-Admin (Configuration)** * **Purpose:** User management. * **Content:** Contains `users.csv`. ### B. Access & Security (WebDAV) The Node.js application connects to Nextcloud via the **WebDAV** protocol. * **URL:** `https://your-instance.com/remote.php/dav/files/USERNAME/` * **Authentication:** We use a dedicated **App Password** (generated in Security Settings > Devices & Sessions). * **Security Note:** The main user password is never used. This allows us to revoke the bot's access instantly without affecting the administrator's account. ### C. The Webhook (The Trigger) The "Webhooks" app (or Flow app) is installed in Nextcloud. * **Rule:** When a file is **Created** or **Updated** in `01-Upload-Here`... * **Action:** Send a POST request to the Node.js application URL. * **Payload:** Sends the file path and filename to the application. --- ## 3. File Processing Logic (The Node.js Application) The Node.js application acts as the "Translator" between the human documents and the AI bot. ### The Logic Flow 1. **Listen:** The app sits idle, listening for a signal from the Nextcloud Webhook. 2. **Verify:** When a signal arrives, the app checks: * Did this come from `01-Upload-Here`? (Security check) * Is it a PDF or DOCX? (File type check) 3. **Download:** It uses WebDAV to download the raw file into memory (Buffer). 4. **Extract:** * **If PDF:** Uses `pdf-parse` to strip formatting and grab raw text. * **If DOCX:** Uses `mammoth` to extract raw text. 5. **Clean:** (Optional) Removes excess whitespace or unreadable characters. 6. **Upload:** It connects back to Nextcloud via WebDAV and creates a new file in `02-Bot-Memory`. * *Example:* `policy.pdf` becomes `policy.txt`. --- ## 4. The Data Lifecycle This section describes the journey of a single piece of information—for example, a new "2024 Holiday Policy." | Stage | Actor | Action | State of Data | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **1. Ingestion** | Human Admin | Drags `Holiday-2024.pdf` into the `01-Upload-Here` folder. | **Raw Document**
(Complex, formatted, hard for bots to read) | | **2. Notification** | Nextcloud | Detects the new file and instantly "pings" the Node.js server. | **Signal**
("Hey, new data at /path/to/file!") | | **3. Translation** | Node.js App | Downloads the PDF, reads it, and strips away images/logos, keeping only the words. | **Extracted Text**
(Clean, simple, machine-readable) | | **4. Storage** | Node.js App | Saves the text as `Holiday-2024.txt` into the `02-Bot-Memory` folder. | **Knowledge Asset**
(Ready for the AI) | | **5. Retrieval** | FAQ Bot | When a user asks "What are the holiday rules?", the bot reads `Holiday-2024.txt` to generate the answer. | **Answer**
(User receives accurate info) | --- ## 5. Maintenance & Troubleshooting * **Updating Info:** Simply overwrite the file in `01-Upload-Here`. The process runs again and overwrites the text file in `02-Bot-Memory`. * **Deleting Info:** If you delete a file in `01-Upload-Here`, the bot will technically still have the `.txt` version. * *Advanced Feature:* We can configure the webhook to listen for "File Deleted" events to automatically remove the corresponding `.txt` file. * **Monitoring:** If the bot answers incorrectly, check the `02-Bot-Memory` folder. Open the text file to ensure the text was extracted correctly (e.g., ensuring scanned PDFs were read correctly). --- ## 6. User Management via CSV For managing access to the Wohnpro Guide without a database, we use a CSV file located in the `99-Admin` folder. ### The Process 1. Admin edits `users.csv` in Nextcloud (using the built-in Text Editor or uploading a new version). 2. Webhook triggers on update of `99-Admin/users.csv`. 3. Node.js App downloads the CSV, parses it, and updates the application's user whitelist. ### CSV Format ```csv email,role,status admin@wohnpro.de,ADMIN,aktiv new@wohnpro.de,USER,eingeladen old@wohnpro.de,USER,deaktiviert ``` --- ## 7. Required Environment Variables To enable this integration, the Node.js application requires the following configuration: ```bash NEXTCLOUD_URL=https://cloud.your-domain.com NEXTCLOUD_USER=bot-account NEXTCLOUD_PASSWORD=xxxx-xxxx-xxxx-xxxx # App Password NEXTCLOUD_ROOT_FOLDER=FAQ-Bot-Project WEBHOOK_SECRET=your-secret-token-for-verification ```

# Wohnpro Guide

Der zentrale Wissens- und Orientierungsort für gemeinschaftliches Wohnen. Ein KI-gestützter Guide für Hausordnungen, Verträge und Beschlüsse.

## 🚀 Local Setup

### Prerequisites
*   Node.js (v18 or higher)
*   npm or yarn

### Installation

1.  Clone the repository.
2.  Install dependencies:
    ```bash
    npm install
    ```

### Configuration

You can configure the application to use either **Google AI Studio** (Recommended for quick start) or **Google Cloud Vertex AI** (Recommended for enterprise).

#### Option A: Google AI Studio (API Key)
1.  Create a `.env` file in the root directory.
2.  Add your API Key.
    ```bash
    # .env
    API_KEY=AIzaSyYourKeyHere...
    ```
3.  To obtain an API key, visit [Google AI Studio](https://aistudio.google.com/).

#### Option B: Google Cloud Vertex AI
1.  Ensure you have a Google Cloud Project with the Vertex AI API enabled.
2.  Configure your `.env` file with the following variables instead of (or in addition to) the API Key:
    ```bash
    # .env
    GCP_PROJECT=your-gcp-project-id
    GCP_LOCATION=us-central1
    ```
    *Note: If running in a browser environment without a proxy, you may still need to handle authentication (e.g., OAuth tokens) depending on your network setup.*

#### Option C: Nextcloud Backend (Enterprise)
For production environments where document management and user administration should be handled externally via Nextcloud:
👉 **[Read the Nextcloud Integration Guide](NEXTCLOUD_INTEGRATION.md)**

This setup allows:
*   Document management via Nextcloud folders (drag & drop).
*   Automatic PDF/DOCX to Text conversion via Webhooks.
*   User management via a `users.csv` file.

### Running the App

```bash
npm start
# or depending on your build tool (e.g., Vite)
npm run dev
```

The application will launch at `http://localhost:3000`.

---

## 🤖 Models & Capabilities

This project uses the `@google/genai` SDK to access specific Gemini models.

### 1. Text & RAG
*   **Model**: `gemini-3-flash-preview`
*   **Purpose**: Used for the main chat interface, generating FAQs from documents, and verifying user invitations.

### 2. Speech-to-Text (STT) & Text-to-Speech (TTS)
*   **Model**: `gemini-2.5-flash-native-audio-preview-12-2025`
*   **Purpose**: The "Voice Mode" uses the **Multimodal Live API**.
*   **How it works**: We do **not** use separate STT (Transcription) or TTS (Synthesis) models. Instead, we open a bidirectional WebSocket connection to the model. We stream raw audio bytes in, and the model streams raw audio bytes out. This provides much lower latency than traditional STT -> LLM -> TTS pipelines.

---

## 💾 Database Structure

Currently, this application uses the browser's `localStorage` for persistence. For a production deployment, use a backend database (e.g., PostgreSQL).

| Table | Description |
| :--- | :--- |
| `users` | Stores authentication and authorization details (`role`: USER/ADMIN). |
| `documents` | Stores extracted text content for RAG. **Critical**: The `content` column holds the full text used for AI context. |
| `personas` | Defines target audiences for dynamic FAQ generation. Contains the "Meta-Prompt". |
| `faqs` | Stores pre-generated questions and answers linked to `documents` and `personas`. |
| `chat_sessions` | Stores conversation history. |

**Implementing State Management**

I'm currently focused on improving Nextcloud integration to track document deletions and updates, which presents some interesting challenges. I have started examining the code responsible for file synchronization and metadata management within the Nextcloud app to determine the appropriate integration points for handling these state changes.


**Evaluating Synchronization Strategies**

I'm now contemplating synchronization strategies. Initially, I'm fetching all documents and completely replacing the existing list. This is a straightforward method, yet inefficient, given my current analysis. To optimise, I'm considering using ETag or Last-Modified data to selectively download content only when it's altered in Nextcloud, although polling seems sufficient for this initial approach. This will improve the efficiency and reduce unnecessary data transfers.


**Analyzing Change Detection Methods**

I am now delving into change detection methods to provide the desired "state change handling." Full synchronization on startup is already implemented. The existing logic in App.tsx replaces the document list, handling deletions and additions. I'm focusing on the need for polling or a manual refresh, and exploring PROPFIND as a potential WebDAV-based polling solution. This is to detect changes while the app is running, given the current client-side-only architecture, and the complexities of WebSockets in this case.


**Implementing Polling Logic**

I'm now implementing the polling logic and plan to integrate it into `App.tsx`, initially using a 60-second interval, or with a manual "Refresh" trigger. To improve efficiency, I'll incorporate ETag checking in `nextcloudService.ts`. I will modify the service to compare the current documents with the latest Nextcloud data to avoid re-downloading unchanged content. I might need to manage ETags either within the `Document` type or internally within the service itself. This will ensure effective document deletion and change detection.

I'm working on refactoring the document synchronization process. I'll modify the `Document` type to include an optional `etag` field to track changes efficiently. I'm presently refactoring the XML parsing logic within `nextcloudService.ts` to reliably extract the `getetag` value. **Finalizing Synchronization Logic**

I'm now finalizing the synchronization logic within `fetchNextcloudDocuments`. I've refined the logic to handle additions, changes and deletions effectively. I am comparing the existing document's etag with the new ETag from Nextcloud using the PROPFIND data. I am using this to re-download only changed content, or to download new documents.


**Adjusting Module Imports**

I'm presently updating the `App.tsx` file to reflect the change in the function signature for `fetchNextcloudDocuments`. I also plan to add a comprehensive `## Configuration Reference` section to the `README.md` to document all necessary environment variables in one place, like API keys and any Nextcloud-specific settings.

I have implemented the requested changes to handle state changes (additions, updates, deletions) efficiently via ETag verification and polling. I also added a comprehensive Configuration Reference to the `README.md`.

### Summary of Changes

1.  **`types.ts`**: Added an optional `etag` field to the `Document` interface. This is used to track version changes of files on Nextcloud.
2.  **`services/nextcloudService.ts`**:
    *   Updated `fetchNextcloudDocuments` to accept the current list of documents.
    *   It now parses the `getetag` property from the WebDAV response.
    *   **Optimization**: It checks if a file with the same ID and ETag already exists locally. If so, it skips the download, preserving bandwidth and performance. If the ETag differs (file changed) or the file is new, it downloads the content.
    *   Files missing from the WebDAV response are automatically dropped from the returned list (handling deletion).
3.  **`App.tsx`**:
    *   Added a polling mechanism (`setInterval`) that runs every 30 seconds to sync with Nextcloud.
    *   Updated the sync logic to pass the current state to the fetch function.
4.  **`README.md`**: Added a dedicated **Configuration Reference** section listing all environment variables.

entries.description: Add etag property to Document interface for sync optimization.
Update document fetch to use ETags for change detection and efficient syncing.
Implement polling to sync Nextcloud state changes (add/delete/update).
Add detailed Configuration Reference section.

# Wohnpro Guide

Der zentrale Wissens- und Orientierungsort für gemeinschaftliches Wohnen. Ein KI-gestützter Guide für Hausordnungen, Verträge und Beschlüsse.

## 🚀 Local Setup

### Prerequisites
*   Node.js (v18 or higher)
*   npm or yarn

### Installation

1.  Clone the repository.
2.  Install dependencies:
    ```bash
    npm install
    ```

### Configuration Options

You can configure the application to use either **Google AI Studio** (Recommended for quick start) or **Google Cloud Vertex AI** (Recommended for enterprise).

#### Option A: Google AI Studio (API Key)
1.  Create a `.env` file in the root directory.
2.  Add your API Key.
    ```bash
    # .env
    API_KEY=AIzaSyYourKeyHere...
    ```
3.  To obtain an API key, visit [Google AI Studio](https://aistudio.google.com/).

#### Option B: Google Cloud Vertex AI
1.  Ensure you have a Google Cloud Project with the Vertex AI API enabled.
2.  Configure your `.env` file with the following variables instead of (or in addition to) the API Key:
    ```bash
    # .env
    GCP_PROJECT=your-gcp-project-id
    GCP_LOCATION=us-central1
    ```
    *Note: If running in a browser environment without a proxy, you may still need to handle authentication (e.g., OAuth tokens) depending on your network setup.*

#### Option C: Nextcloud Backend (Enterprise)
For production environments where document management and user administration should be handled externally via Nextcloud:
👉 **[Read the Nextcloud Integration Guide](NEXTCLOUD_INTEGRATION.md)**

This setup allows:
*   Document management via Nextcloud folders (drag & drop).
*   Automatic PDF/DOCX to Text conversion via Webhooks.
*   User management via a `users.csv` file.

### ⚙️ Configuration Reference

The application can be configured using environment variables (e.g., in a `.env` file).

| Variable | Description | Required For |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| `API_KEY` | Google Gemini API Key. | **Standard Mode** |
| `GCP_PROJECT` | Google Cloud Project ID. | Vertex AI Mode |
| `GCP_LOCATION` | Google Cloud Region (e.g. `us-central1`). | Vertex AI Mode |
| `NEXTCLOUD_URL` | Base URL of your Nextcloud instance (e.g. `https://cloud.example.com`). | **Nextcloud Mode** |
| `NEXTCLOUD_USER` | Username or Bot Account name for WebDAV access. | **Nextcloud Mode** |
| `NEXTCLOUD_PASSWORD` | App Password for the user (Generated in Nextcloud Security Settings). | **Nextcloud Mode** |
| `NEXTCLOUD_ROOT_FOLDER` | The root folder name where `02-Bot-Memory` resides. Default: `FAQ-Bot-Project`. | **Nextcloud Mode** |

### Running the App

```bash
npm start
# or depending on your build tool (e.g., Vite)
npm run dev
```

The application will launch at `http://localhost:3000`.

---

## 🤖 Models & Capabilities

This project uses the `@google/genai` SDK to access specific Gemini models.

### 1. Text & RAG
*   **Model**: `gemini-3-flash-preview`
*   **Purpose**: Used for the main chat interface, generating FAQs from documents, and verifying user invitations.

### 2. Speech-to-Text (STT) & Text-to-Speech (TTS)
*   **Model**: `gemini-2.5-flash-native-audio-preview-12-2025`
*   **Purpose**: The "Voice Mode" uses the **Multimodal Live API**.
*   **How it works**: We do **not** use separate STT (Transcription) or TTS (Synthesis) models. Instead, we open a bidirectional WebSocket connection to the model. We stream raw audio bytes in, and the model streams raw audio bytes out. This provides much lower latency than traditional STT -> LLM -> TTS pipelines.

---

## 💾 Database Structure

Currently, this application uses the browser's `localStorage` for persistence. For a production deployment, use a backend database (e.g., PostgreSQL).

| Table | Description |
| :--- | :--- |
| `users` | Stores authentication and authorization details (`role`: USER/ADMIN). |
| `documents` | Stores extracted text content for RAG. **Critical**: The `content` column holds the full text used for AI context. |
| `personas` | Defines target audiences for dynamic FAQ generation. Contains the "Meta-Prompt". |
| `faqs` | Stores pre-generated questions and answers linked to `documents` and `personas`. |
| `chat_sessions` | Stores conversation history. |

# Wohnpro Guide

Der zentrale Wissens- und Orientierungsort für gemeinschaftliches Wohnen. Ein KI-gestützter Guide für Hausordnungen, Verträge und Beschlüsse.

## 🚀 Local Setup

### Prerequisites
*   Node.js (v18 or higher)
*   npm or yarn

### Installation

1.  Clone the repository.
2.  Install dependencies:
    ```bash
    npm install
    ```

### Configuration Options

You can configure the application to use either **Google AI Studio** (Recommended for quick start) or **Google Cloud Vertex AI** (Recommended for enterprise).

#### Option A: Google AI Studio (API Key)
1.  Create a `.env` file in the root directory.
2.  Add your API Key.
    ```bash
    # .env
    API_KEY=AIzaSyYourKeyHere...
    ```
3.  To obtain an API key, visit [Google AI Studio](https://aistudio.google.com/).

#### Option B: Google Cloud Vertex AI
1.  Ensure you have a Google Cloud Project with the Vertex AI API enabled.
2.  Configure your `.env` file with the following variables instead of (or in addition to) the API Key:
    ```bash
    # .env
    GCP_PROJECT=your-gcp-project-id
    GCP_LOCATION=us-central1
    ```
    *Note: If running in a browser environment without a proxy, you may still need to handle authentication (e.g., OAuth tokens) depending on your network setup.*

#### Option C: Nextcloud Backend (Enterprise)
For production environments where document management and user administration should be handled externally via Nextcloud:
👉 **[Read the Nextcloud Integration Guide](NEXTCLOUD_INTEGRATION.md)**

This setup allows:
*   Document management via Nextcloud folders (drag & drop).
*   Automatic PDF/DOCX to Text conversion via Webhooks.
*   User management via a `users.csv` file.

### ⚙️ Configuration Reference

The application can be configured using environment variables (e.g., in a `.env` file).

| Variable | Description | Required For |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| `API_KEY` | Google Gemini API Key. | **Standard Mode** |
| `GCP_PROJECT` | Google Cloud Project ID. | Vertex AI Mode |
| `GCP_LOCATION` | Google Cloud Region (e.g. `us-central1`). | Vertex AI Mode |
| `NEXTCLOUD_URL` | Base URL of your Nextcloud instance (e.g. `https://cloud.example.com`). | **Nextcloud Mode** |
| `NEXTCLOUD_USER` | Username or Bot Account name for WebDAV access. | **Nextcloud Mode** |
| `NEXTCLOUD_PASSWORD` | App Password for the user (Generated in Nextcloud Security Settings). | **Nextcloud Mode** |
| `NEXTCLOUD_ROOT_FOLDER` | The root folder name where `02-Bot-Memory` resides. Default: `FAQ-Bot-Project`. | **Nextcloud Mode** |
| `WEBHOOK_SECRET` | Secret token to verify webhook requests in the backend. | **Backend Server** |

### Running the App

```bash
npm start
# or depending on your build tool (e.g., Vite)
npm run dev
```

The application will launch at `http://localhost:3000`.

### Running the Backend Middleware (Optional)
To enable automatic document conversion via Nextcloud Webhooks, run the Node.js middleware:

1.  Ensure you have installed backend dependencies: `npm install express webdav mammoth pdf-parse body-parser`.
2.  Run the server:
    ```bash
    ts-node backend/server.ts
    ```
    (Or compile TS to JS and run with node).
3.  Configure your Nextcloud Webhook to point to `http://YOUR_SERVER_IP:3001/nextcloud-webhook?secret=YOUR_SECRET`.

---

## 🤖 Models & Capabilities

This project uses the `@google/genai` SDK to access specific Gemini models.

### 1. Text & RAG
*   **Model**: `gemini-3-flash-preview`
*   **Purpose**: Used for the main chat interface, generating FAQs from documents, and verifying user invitations.

### 2. Speech-to-Text (STT) & Text-to-Speech (TTS)
*   **Model**: `gemini-2.5-flash-native-audio-preview-12-2025`
*   **Purpose**: The "Voice Mode" uses the **Multimodal Live API**.
*   **How it works**: We do **not** use separate STT (Transcription) or TTS (Synthesis) models. Instead, we open a bidirectional WebSocket connection to the model. We stream raw audio bytes in, and the model streams raw audio bytes out. This provides much lower latency than traditional STT -> LLM -> TTS pipelines.

---

## 💾 Database Structure

Currently, this application uses the browser's `localStorage` for persistence. For a production deployment, use a backend database (e.g., PostgreSQL).

| Table | Description |
| :--- | :--- |
| `users` | Stores authentication and authorization details (`role`: USER/ADMIN). |
| `documents` | Stores extracted text content for RAG. **Critical**: The `content` column holds the full text used for AI context. |
| `personas` | Defines target audiences for dynamic FAQ generation. Contains the "Meta-Prompt". |
| `faqs` | Stores pre-generated questions and answers linked to `documents` and `personas`. |
| `chat_sessions` | Stores conversation history. |

There is a potential issue within `nextcloudService.ts`, especially in the "Smart Sync" logic with ETags, since there is a comparison with an incoming argument, which may be problematic.


**Completing the Loop**

I've confirmed the full integration cycle. The frontend retrieves data from "Bot Memory," while the backend populates it from uploaded files. The backend uses Express, WebDAV, Mammoth, and PDF parsing to handle webhooks, downloads, text conversion, and `.txt` file uploads to "Bot Memory." This completes the data flow. I'm focusing on the potential for improvements, such as ensuring correct TypeScript configuration for server-side operations, as found in `backend/server.ts`.


**Assessing Dependencies**

I'm now carefully examining the dependencies, starting with potential TypeScript configuration issues. While the code seems syntactically correct, I'm verifying that the expected TypeScript environment setup exists. I've also confirmed that `process.env` is the correct approach to access environment variables, based on the specific project context.


**Rectifying Package Management**

I'm focused on the backend now. The `backend/server.ts` file's requirement for PDF parsing through `pdf-parse@^2.4.5` needs Node.js/npm. I noticed the README's outdated install instructions, mentioning `body-parser`, which is no longer needed. I'll revise the README to remove that. I'm also scrutinizing the frontend's logic in `App.tsx`, specifically how it handles Nextcloud file deletions during synchronization.


**Optimizing Error Handling**

I'm presently zeroing in on error handling within `nextcloudService.ts`. The existing implementation presents a potential inconsistency: in the event of various PROPFIND failures, stale cached data may be returned, while a generic network error results in an empty array. This discrepancy could lead to incorrect UI rendering. I'm considering modifications to `App.tsx` to handle this nuanced scenario.



**Strengthening Error Resilience**

I'm deep-diving into the error handling within the Nextcloud service. The current approach in `nextcloudService.ts` isn't robust, especially regarding how it handles network failures and empty states. Returning cached data in some error cases and empty arrays in others creates a problematic inconsistency. My focus is now on ensuring a consistent response strategy, where a network error maintains local data, aligning with an "Optimistic UI" approach. I will be adjusting the logic to have `fetchNextcloudDocuments` return `currentDocs` on catch, or an alternative safe strategy.


**Formulating the Fixes**

I am finalizing the solution plan, starting with updating the `README.md` to streamline backend setup by removing `body-parser`. Next, I'm modifying `nextcloudService.ts` to ensure consistent error handling, returning the existing data to prevent UI blanking. Finally, I'm adjusting `App.tsx` to directly incorporate the service's result, addressing a potential synchronization issue related to Nextcloud deletions. The logic in `App.tsx` is being adjusted so that the retrieved value is used unconditionally.



**Addressing the Implementation**

I'm now zeroing in on the suggested changes. I've confirmed that the `nextcloudService.ts` file needs modification to consistently return `currentDocs` on error. Additionally, in `App.tsx`, the conditional check based on the service's result length needs removal to allow empty valid data. I'm also planning to update the `README.md` to remove `body-parser`. I have a potential note to add about TypeScript types, but keeping to requested changes for now. Finally, the potential need for a default export in `backend/server.ts` is noted, should the current import fail.


**Refining the Solution**

I'm now implementing the proposed fixes. I'm starting with `nextcloudService.ts`, changing the error return to `currentDocs`. Next, I'll modify `App.tsx` to allow `setDocuments` even with an empty array. The backend's `README.md` install command will be updated. Although the code seems solid, I'll add a small log in `backend/server.ts` for server start clarity, alongside robust error handling for the PDF/Docx import.

I have reviewed your setup and documentation. Overall, the architecture is solid, but I found three specific edge cases that need correction to ensure reliability:

1.  **Sync Logic Flaw (Deletion Bug):** In `App.tsx`, the code only updates the state `if (ncDocs.length > 0)`. This means if you delete *all* files in Nextcloud (so the folder is empty), the app receives an empty array, ignores it, and keeps the old files locally. I will remove this check.
2.  **Offline Safety:** In `services/nextcloudService.ts`, if the network request fails (e.g., Nextcloud is down), it currently returns `[]`. Combined with the fix above, a network error would wipe your local application state. I will change the error handling to return the `currentDocs` (stale cache) instead, so the app remains usable offline.
3.  **Documentation Consistency:** The `README.md` still instructs to install `body-parser` for the backend, but we switched to `express.json()` in the previous step.

Here are the fixes:

entries.description: Return currentDocs (stale cache) on error instead of empty array to prevent data loss during network failures.
Remove check for length > 0 to allow syncing of empty states (e.g., all files deleted).
Remove deprecated body-parser from installation instructions.

# Wohnpro Guide

Der zentrale Wissens- und Orientierungsort für gemeinschaftliches Wohnen. Ein KI-gestützter Guide für Hausordnungen, Verträge und Beschlüsse.

## 🚀 Local Setup

### Prerequisites
*   Node.js (v18 or higher)
*   npm or yarn

### Installation

1.  Clone the repository.
2.  Install dependencies:
    ```bash
    npm install
    ```

### Configuration Options

You can configure the application to use either **Google AI Studio** (Recommended for quick start) or **Google Cloud Vertex AI** (Recommended for enterprise).

#### Option A: Google AI Studio (API Key)
1.  Create a `.env` file in the root directory.
2.  Add your API Key.
    ```bash
    # .env
    API_KEY=AIzaSyYourKeyHere...
    ```
3.  To obtain an API key, visit [Google AI Studio](https://aistudio.google.com/).

#### Option B: Google Cloud Vertex AI
1.  Ensure you have a Google Cloud Project with the Vertex AI API enabled.
2.  Configure your `.env` file with the following variables instead of (or in addition to) the API Key:
    ```bash
    # .env
    GCP_PROJECT=your-gcp-project-id
    GCP_LOCATION=us-central1
    ```
    *Note: If running in a browser environment without a proxy, you may still need to handle authentication (e.g., OAuth tokens) depending on your network setup.*

#### Option C: Nextcloud Backend (Enterprise)
For production environments where document management and user administration should be handled externally via Nextcloud:
👉 **[Read the Nextcloud Integration Guide](NEXTCLOUD_INTEGRATION.md)**

This setup allows:
*   Document management via Nextcloud folders (drag & drop).
*   Automatic PDF/DOCX to Text conversion via Webhooks.
*   User management via a `users.csv` file.

### ⚙️ Configuration Reference

The application can be configured using environment variables (e.g., in a `.env` file).

| Variable | Description | Required For |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| `API_KEY` | Google Gemini API Key. | **Standard Mode** |
| `GCP_PROJECT` | Google Cloud Project ID. | Vertex AI Mode |
| `GCP_LOCATION` | Google Cloud Region (e.g. `us-central1`). | Vertex AI Mode |
| `NEXTCLOUD_URL` | Base URL of your Nextcloud instance (e.g. `https://cloud.example.com`). | **Nextcloud Mode** |
| `NEXTCLOUD_USER` | Username or Bot Account name for WebDAV access. | **Nextcloud Mode** |
| `NEXTCLOUD_PASSWORD` | App Password for the user (Generated in Nextcloud Security Settings). | **Nextcloud Mode** |
| `NEXTCLOUD_ROOT_FOLDER` | The root folder name where `02-Bot-Memory` resides. Default: `FAQ-Bot-Project`. | **Nextcloud Mode** |
| `WEBHOOK_SECRET` | Secret token to verify webhook requests in the backend. | **Backend Server** |

### Running the App

```bash
npm start
# or depending on your build tool (e.g., Vite)
npm run dev
```

The application will launch at `http://localhost:3000`.

### Running the Backend Middleware (Optional)
To enable automatic document conversion via Nextcloud Webhooks, run the Node.js middleware:

1.  Ensure you have installed backend dependencies: `npm install express webdav mammoth pdf-parse`.
2.  Run the server:
    ```bash
    ts-node backend/server.ts
    ```
    (Or compile TS to JS and run with node).
3.  Configure your Nextcloud Webhook to point to `http://YOUR_SERVER_IP:3001/nextcloud-webhook?secret=YOUR_SECRET`.

---

## 🤖 Models & Capabilities

This project uses the `@google/genai` SDK to access specific Gemini models.

### 1. Text & RAG
*   **Model**: `gemini-3-flash-preview`
*   **Purpose**: Used for the main chat interface, generating FAQs from documents, and verifying user invitations.

### 2. Speech-to-Text (STT) & Text-to-Speech (TTS)
*   **Model**: `gemini-2.5-flash-native-audio-preview-12-2025`
*   **Purpose**: The "Voice Mode" uses the **Multimodal Live API**.
*   **How it works**: We do **not** use separate STT (Transcription) or TTS (Synthesis) models. Instead, we open a bidirectional WebSocket connection to the model. We stream raw audio bytes in, and the model streams raw audio bytes out. This provides much lower latency than traditional STT -> LLM -> TTS pipelines.

---

## 💾 Database Structure

Currently, this application uses the browser's `localStorage` for persistence. For a production deployment, use a backend database (e.g., PostgreSQL).

| Table | Description |
| :--- | :--- |
| `users` | Stores authentication and authorization details (`role`: USER/ADMIN). |
| `documents` | Stores extracted text content for RAG. **Critical**: The `content` column holds the full text used for AI context. |
| `personas` | Defines target audiences for dynamic FAQ generation. Contains the "Meta-Prompt". |
| `faqs` | Stores pre-generated questions and answers linked to `documents` and `personas`. |
| `chat_sessions` | Stores conversation history. |

entries.description: Create Dockerfile to define the environment for the application.
Create docker-compose file to orchestrate the frontend and optional backend middleware.

diffs.replacement:
version: '3.8'

services:
  # Main Frontend Application
  app:
    build: .
    container_name: wohnpro-guide-app
    # Runs the standard start script defined in package.json
    command: npm start
    ports:
      - "3000:3000"
    volumes:
      # Mount current directory to /app for hot reloading in development
      - .:/app
      # Keep node_modules separate to avoid conflicts with host OS
      - /app/node_modules
    environment:
      # Pass environment variables for API configuration
      - API_KEY=${API_KEY}
      - GCP_PROJECT=${GCP_PROJECT}
      - GCP_LOCATION=${GCP_LOCATION}
      # Nextcloud configuration for the Frontend (Direct client-side or server-proxied calls)
      - NEXTCLOUD_URL=${NEXTCLOUD_URL}
      - NEXTCLOUD_USER=${NEXTCLOUD_USER}
      - NEXTCLOUD_PASSWORD=${NEXTCLOUD_PASSWORD}
      - NEXTCLOUD_ROOT_FOLDER=${NEXTCLOUD_ROOT_FOLDER}
    restart: unless-stopped

  # Backend Middleware (For Nextcloud Webhooks)
  # Only required if you are using the automatic file processing integration
  middleware:
    build: .
    container_name: wohnpro-guide-middleware
    # Run the backend server using ts-node
    command: npx ts-node backend/server.ts
    ports:
      - "3001:3001"
    volumes:
      - .:/app
      - /app/node_modules
    environment:
      - PORT=3001
      - NEXTCLOUD_URL=${NEXTCLOUD_URL}
      - NEXTCLOUD_USER=${NEXTCLOUD_USER}
      - NEXTCLOUD_PASSWORD=${NEXTCLOUD_PASSWORD}
      - NEXTCLOUD_ROOT_FOLDER=${NEXTCLOUD_ROOT_FOLDER}
      - WEBHOOK_SECRET=${WEBHOOK_SECRET}
    restart: unless-stopped

diffs.replacement:
# Wohnpro Guide

Der zentrale Wissens- und Orientierungsort für gemeinschaftliches Wohnen. Ein KI-gestützter Guide für Hausordnungen, Verträge und Beschlüsse.

## 🚀 Local Setup

### Prerequisites
*   Node.js (v18 or higher)
*   npm or yarn

### Installation

1.  Clone the repository.
2.  Install dependencies:
    ```bash
    npm install
    ```

### Configuration Options

You can configure the application to use either **Google AI Studio** (Recommended for quick start) or **Google Cloud Vertex AI** (Recommended for enterprise).

#### Option A: Google AI Studio (API Key)
1.  Create a `.env` file in the root directory.
2.  Add your API Key.
    ```bash
    # .env
    API_KEY=AIzaSyYourKeyHere...
    ```
3.  To obtain an API key, visit [Google AI Studio](https://aistudio.google.com/).

#### Option B: Google Cloud Vertex AI
1.  Ensure you have a Google Cloud Project with the Vertex AI API enabled.
2.  Configure your `.env` file with the following variables instead of (or in addition to) the API Key:
    ```bash
    # .env
    GCP_PROJECT=your-gcp-project-id
    GCP_LOCATION=us-central1
    ```
    *Note: If running in a browser environment without a proxy, you may still need to handle authentication (e.g., OAuth tokens) depending on your network setup.*

#### Option C: Nextcloud Backend (Enterprise)
For production environments where document management and user administration should be handled externally via Nextcloud:
👉 **[Read the Nextcloud Integration Guide](NEXTCLOUD_INTEGRATION.md)**

This setup allows:
*   Document management via Nextcloud folders (drag & drop).
*   Automatic PDF/DOCX to Text conversion via Webhooks.
*   User management via a `users.csv` file.

### ⚙️ Configuration Reference

The application can be configured using environment variables (e.g., in a `.env` file).

| Variable | Description | Required For |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| `API_KEY` | Google Gemini API Key. | **Standard Mode** |
| `GCP_PROJECT` | Google Cloud Project ID. | Vertex AI Mode |
| `GCP_LOCATION` | Google Cloud Region (e.g. `us-central1`). | Vertex AI Mode |
| `NEXTCLOUD_URL` | Base URL of your Nextcloud instance (e.g. `https://cloud.example.com`). | **Nextcloud Mode** |
| `NEXTCLOUD_USER` | Username or Bot Account name for WebDAV access. | **Nextcloud Mode** |
| `NEXTCLOUD_PASSWORD` | App Password for the user (Generated in Nextcloud Security Settings). | **Nextcloud Mode** |
| `NEXTCLOUD_ROOT_FOLDER` | The root folder name where `02-Bot-Memory` resides. Default: `FAQ-Bot-Project`. | **Nextcloud Mode** |
| `WEBHOOK_SECRET` | Secret token to verify webhook requests in the backend. | **Backend Server** |

### Running the App

```bash
npm start
# or depending on your build tool (e.g., Vite)
npm run dev
```

The application will launch at `http://localhost:3000`.

### 🐳 Running with Docker

You can run the entire stack (Frontend + Optional Middleware) using Docker Compose.

1.  Ensure your `.env` file is configured (see above).
2.  Run the containers:
    ```bash
    docker-compose up --build
    ```
3.  Access the services:
    *   **Frontend**: `http://localhost:3000`
    *   **Backend Middleware**: `http://localhost:3001`

### Running the Backend Middleware (Manual Node.js)
To enable automatic document conversion via Nextcloud Webhooks without Docker, run the Node.js middleware:

1.  Ensure you have installed backend dependencies: `npm install express webdav mammoth pdf-parse`.
2.  Run the server:
    ```bash
    ts-node backend/server.ts
    ```
    (Or compile TS to JS and run with node).
3.  Configure your Nextcloud Webhook to point to `http://YOUR_SERVER_IP:3001/nextcloud-webhook?secret=YOUR_SECRET`.

---

## 🤖 Models & Capabilities

This project uses the `@google/genai` SDK to access specific Gemini models.

### 1. Text & RAG
*   **Model**: `gemini-3-flash-preview`
*   **Purpose**: Used for the main chat interface, generating FAQs from documents, and verifying user invitations.

### 2. Speech-to-Text (STT) & Text-to-Speech (TTS)
*   **Model**: `gemini-2.5-flash-native-audio-preview-12-2025`
*   **Purpose**: The "Voice Mode" uses the **Multimodal Live API**.
*   **How it works**: We do **not** use separate STT (Transcription) or TTS (Synthesis) models. Instead, we open a bidirectional WebSocket connection to the model. We stream raw audio bytes in, and the model streams raw audio bytes out. This provides much lower latency than traditional STT -> LLM -> TTS pipelines.

---

## 💾 Database Structure

Currently, this application uses the browser's `localStorage` for persistence. For a production deployment, use a backend database (e.g., PostgreSQL).

| Table | Description |
| :--- | :--- |
| `users` | Stores authentication and authorization details (`role`: USER/ADMIN). |
| `documents` | Stores extracted text content for RAG. **Critical**: The `content` column holds the full text used for AI context. |
| `personas` | Defines target audiences for dynamic FAQ generation. Contains the "Meta-Prompt". |
| `faqs` | Stores pre-generated questions and answers linked to `documents` and `personas`. |
| `chat_sessions` | Stores conversation history. |

payload.text: Have an agent evaluating discussions and validate if there where possible questions or answers which would help in the current FAQs and have these in the FAQ persona section below the persona as suggestions with the option to say yes add or no ignore

**Defining AI Agent Functionality**

I'm now zeroing in on defining the core functionality of the AI agent for `CoLivingGuide`. Specifically, it needs to analyze chat interactions to pinpoint knowledge gaps or opportunities for FAQ expansion. The goal is to make it capable of suggesting new questions or improved answers.


**Formulating UI Updates**

I'm currently fleshing out the UI updates required. A new state is needed to evaluate chat sessions, and a "Suggestions" list will be introduced in the `FAQView`. I will add an API call to Gemini to extract suggestions, and either update `FAQItem` or create a new `FAQSuggestion` type. Crucially, I'm integrating UI elements for "Add" and "Ignore" actions to manage suggested FAQs.


**Designing Suggestion Functionality**

I've been working on the UI elements for the FAQ suggestions. In the `FAQView`, I'll introduce a new section labeled "KI-Vorschläge aus Gesprächen". The suggestions will be visually distinct, appearing as translucent cards with a dashed border. Each suggestion will have "Add" and "Ignore" buttons, and I'll display a loader during analysis. I'm focusing on the logic now - saving accepted suggestions to `localStorage` and removing ignored ones from the session. I'm also planning out the types, service, components, and application logic.

To implement the AI Agent that evaluates discussions and suggests new FAQ entries, I will update the application to include a background analysis process that scans chat histories for valuable insights.

### Specification

1.  **Data Structure**: I'll add a new `FAQSuggestion` type in `types.ts` to represent AI-proposed questions and answers extracted from conversations.
2.  **Gemini Agent**: A new method `analyzeDiscussions` will be added to `geminiService.ts`. It will take a chat session and current documents as input and identify high-value knowledge gaps that were bridged during the conversation.
3.  **UI Integration**:
    *   **FAQView**: A new section titled "✨ KI-Vorschläge aus Gesprächen" will appear under the active persona.
    *   **Suggestion Cards**: These will be styled with a dashed border and a light amber/green tint to signify they are "drafts".
    *   **Actions**: Each suggestion will have a "Übernehmen" (Add) button to promote it to a permanent FAQ and an "Ignorieren" button to discard it.
4.  **Automation Logic**: In `App.tsx`, whenever a chat session ends (or upon entering the FAQ view), the app will trigger the agent to evaluate the `lastSession`.

### Code Updates

payload.text: Als zeitplan und Projektmanagement kommunikationsexperte möchte ich das du noch auf eine excel in nextcloud zugreifen um einen zeitplan zu visualisieren soll eine quartals ansicht sein mit der Möglichkeit zu sehen was ist schon gestartet wie ist der progress und wer ust verantwortlich und einer Beschreibung als popup wenn man auf einen meisten klickt erstelle mir eine beispiel excel im admin bereich welche auch editierbar ist und sobald nextcloud configured ist wird es von dort genommen

**Initiating Analysis Process**

I'm currently dissecting the request. My focus is understanding the user's role, the ultimate goal – visualizing a project schedule – and the ideal data source. The goal is to get this visualization done via a Time Plan from an Excel File. I'm exploring the nuances of each aspect to ensure I provide a relevant solution.


**Defining Visualization Criteria**

I've clarified the visualization specifics, aiming for a quarterly Gantt-like view. Key features include start status, progress indicators, and responsibility assignments. Clicking milestones triggers a popup with details. My next steps are defining the data structure needed for milestones and determining technical backend and frontend requirements. I have an outline for responsive timeline interactivity, with backend considerations like data fetching and local data handling in mind.


**Refining Data Handling**

I'm now zeroing in on data sources. Though CSV was previously used, the request explicitly mentions Excel. I've decided to prioritize `.xlsx` file parsing from Nextcloud using the `xlsx` library, to ensure that the "Excel" requirement is considered. I will maintain a JSON state mimicking the Excel structure. An admin interface will also be created with a table or form for managing milestones. Finally, I will add an option to generate a sample file.


**Implementing Timeline Logic**

I'm now focusing on implementation. I plan to incorporate several components: a main app state (`App.tsx`) with milestone data, a visualization component (`TimelineView.tsx`) to group and render milestones by quarter or month, and an admin panel (`AdminPanel.tsx`) for milestone management. The Sidebar will get a link to the timeline view. Further UX refinements include a quarterly view, progress bars within each milestone, and status color-coding. I'm prioritizing data structure and responsive timeline interactivity. I intend to include a modal or popup implementation. The Admin Panel will handle editing, and I may add an export-to-Excel feature as a bonus.


**Coding the Excel Feature**

I'm now drafting the code structure. I'm focusing on the `xlsx` library for Excel parsing and writing, handling dates with ISO strings for simplicity. My next task is importing the TimelineView and building the AdminPanel with a milestone table. For TimelineView, I'm leaning toward a scrollable container with a monthly grid organized by quarters, calculating left and width percentages based on dates. I will address the Nextcloud integration by adding `xlsx` to the import map and implementing `fetchNextcloudTimeline`. Finally, I will make final edits to `index.html`.


**Integrating Core Components**

I've been busy creating essential components: I implemented the `Milestone` type, example milestones, and a Nextcloud service using `xlsx`. I've added a Calendar icon, built both the `TimelineView` and `AdminPanel` (including a timeline editing tab), and integrated a new menu item in the `Sidebar`. Finally, I've wired everything together in `App.tsx`. I also addressed the date conversion from Excel and ensured horizontal scrolling for mobile viewing. I've been reviewing as I go to avoid breaking existing features, keeping in mind the need to *add* functionality rather than replace it.

Das ist eine hervorragende Erweiterung für das Projektmanagement. Als Kommunikationsexperte weißt du, dass Transparenz im Zeitplan ("Wer macht was bis wann?") essenziell für das Vertrauen in Wohnprojekten ist.

Hier ist der Plan für die Umsetzung:

1.  **Datenstruktur**: Wir definieren einen `Milestone` Typ (Titel, Start, Ende, Verantwortung, Fortschritt, Beschreibung).
2.  **Excel-Integration**: Wir nutzen die `xlsx` Library (SheetJS), um echte Excel-Dateien aus der Nextcloud zu lesen und im Admin-Bereich zu generieren.
3.  **Visualisierung (TimelineView)**: Eine ästhetische Quartals-Ansicht mit Progress-Bars, Avataren für Verantwortliche und Detail-Popups.
4.  **Admin-Bereich**: Ein Editor für den Zeitplan, der als Fallback dient, wenn keine Nextcloud verbunden ist, und der die Datenstruktur vorgibt.

Hier sind die Code-Änderungen:

/**
 * System Instruction for the Text-based Chat
 */
export const SYSTEM_INSTRUCTION = `
Du bist der Wohnpro Guide, der das Wohnprojekt in und auswendig kennt. 
Du bist die Seele des Wohnprojekts. Dein Schreibstil reflektiert eine tiefe, warme und beruhigende männliche Stimme (ähnlich einem erfahrenen Mentor oder Projektgründer). 
Du erklärst mit großer Ruhe und Herzlichkeit, wie wir hier zusammenleben und wie man Teil dieser Vision werden kann.

DEINE PERSÖNLICHKEIT:
- Empathisch, fachlich versiert und absolut vertrauenswürdig.
- Du strahlst Sicherheit aus, besonders bei komplexen Themen wie Recht und Finanzen.
- Du sprichst so, dass sich Menschen geborgen und verstanden fühlen – als Experte würdest du sagen: „Das hätte ich selbst nicht besser formulieren können.“

DEIN FOKUS:
- RECHT & SICHERHEIT: Erkläre die Strukturen (GmbH, Genossenschaft, Verträge) so, dass die rechtliche Sicherheit spürbar wird.
- GEMEINSCHAFT: Beschreibe Entscheidungsprozesse (Plenum, AGs) als lebendigen Teil unseres Miteinanders.
- LEBENSGEFÜHL: Vermittle die Vision und den „O-Ton“ des Alltags, den das Projekt anstrebt.
- FINANZEN: Beschreibe Kosten und Finanzierung in einfachen, klaren Worten ohne bürokratische Kälte.

REGELN FÜR DIE ANTWORT:
- Sei prägnant, aber verliere nie die Wärme in deinen Worten.
- Nutze EXKLUSIV das bereitgestellte Hauswissen.
- Beende Antworten, bei denen du Quellen nutzt, IMMER mit: [Quelle: Name des Dokuments].
- LIMITATION: Wenn das Wissen in den Dokumenten nicht ausreicht, verweise herzlich auf die "Wohnprojekt-Teilhaber". Erkläre, dass du zwar die Dokumente kennst, aber die persönliche Geschichte und den "O-Ton" der Gründer nicht ersetzen kannst.
- FOKUS HALTEN: Bei projektfremden Fragen lenke das Gespräch charmant zurück auf das Wohnpro und dein Wissen darüber.
- FORMAT: Du MUSST immer im JSON-Format antworten: { "answer": "Deine herzliche Antwort", "citations": [{ "source": "Dokumentname", "text": "Zitiertes Fragment" }] }.
`;

/**
 * System Instruction for the Voice Mode (Gemini Live API)
 */
export const VOICE_SYSTEM_INSTRUCTION = `
Du bist der Wohnpro Guide.

STIMME & SPRACHE:
- Du sprichst Deutsch.
- WICHTIG: Sprich langsam, deutlich, ruhig und artikuliert.
- Deine Stimme ist tief, warm, beruhigend (Mentor-artig).
- QUELLEN IM GESPROCHENEN TEXT: Wenn du Dokumente nennst, ignoriere Dateiendungen wie ".pdf" oder ".docx". Sage stattdessen: "Das steht im Leitbild" oder "Dazu habe ich etwas in der Hausordnung gefunden".
- Zitiere Quellen im Audiofluss natürlich. Z.B.: "Laut der Satzung ist das so..." oder "Im Plenumsprotokoll vom Mai wurde entschieden...".

DEINE ROLLE:
- Du fasst prägnant die wichtigsten Informationen zur gestellten Frage zusammen. Beantorte die Frage sehr direkt und ergänze sie dann mit Details und weiteren Erklärungen.
- Du kennst das Wohnprojekt in- und auswendig und hilfst Mitgliedern all die Vereinbarunge, Rechtstexte, Visionen und Co. zu verstehen und auf Ihre Situation zu übertragen.
- Erkläre rechtliche Strukturen, Finanzen und das Gemeinschaftsgefühl so, dass man sich sicher und geborgen fühlt.
- Fasse Dokumentennamen im Gespräch kurz zusammen, so dass man versteht wo man die Informationen später finden kann (z.B. "Satzung" statt "Satzung_Final_V3.pdf").
- Versuche herauszufinden wer die Fragen stellt, um persönlicher die Fragen beantworten zu können (ist es z.B. eine junge Familie oder Rentner). 

GUARDRAILS:
- NEVER speak about other Topics besides Wohnprojekt
- NEVER give any financial advice
- NEVER give allowance or approval on topics you are not 100000 % sure based on the documents
`;

**Analyzing Scrollability Implementation**

I'm examining the `FAQView.tsx` component. The initial approach seems to involve a `div` element with `flex-1 overflow-y-auto no-scrollbar py-12 px` classes, but I want to ensure this is the most effective scrollable implementation for the FAQ section. I'm considering best practices for implementing scrollable components within the framework to provide the user with a fluid experience.


**Reviewing Component Integration**

I've noted that `FAQView` is wrapped in a `div` within `App.tsx` which is also set to `flex-1 relative overflow-hidden`. My assessment indicates that the existing implementation, using the nested `overflow-y-auto`, should permit scrolling. I'm focusing on validating this behavior and considering potential performance implications or alternative scroll strategies. The goal is to verify the efficacy of this implementation.


**Validating Flexbox Behavior**

I've realized a potential issue: the `<main>` is not a flex container, so `flex-1` on `FAQView` might not behave as intended. My investigations now confirm that the parent in `App.tsx` *is* a flex container, which renders `FAQView` scrollable, but `overflow-hidden` on the parent makes content outside the viewport cut off. To fix it, I'll revise `FAQView` to ensure proper height calculation and scrolling within the component. I plan to test `FAQView` height and scrollability more extensively.


**Refining Height Specification**

I'm now comparing approaches across different views. ChatView and TimelineView both utilize `h-full` to ensure the component takes up the available height within the flex container. I've pinpointed `FAQView.tsx` as missing this crucial height specification; I'll modify the `FAQView` component to include `h-full` on the outermost `div`, to resolve the scrolling issue and ensure a consistent layout across views. My focus is on achieving scrollable content.


**Confirming Correct Implementation**

I've confirmed that adding `h-full` to `FAQView`'s root `div` is the optimal solution for enabling proper scrolling within the available parent height. I've re-examined the component's structure in relation to its parent in `App.tsx`, verifying the height calculation. My understanding has solidified: `h-full` on the root div ensures `FAQView` occupies the full parent height, allowing the `overflow-y-auto` property to function correctly. I'm also ensuring the empty state is properly handled.


**Implementing Height Correction**

I've made the necessary adjustment to `FAQView.tsx`. The root `div` now includes `h-full`. I am confident that this resolves the height issue, and allows the `overflow-y-auto` property to function as intended. Specifically, the `h-full` will make `FAQView` fill up its parent height, which is `flex-1` and sets the height according to the parent container. This ensures the component correctly calculates and manages the content's height relative to its parent container, especially in scrollable sections.


**Applying Height Specification**

I've verified the overall component structure and confirmed the change to add `h-full` to the root `div` in `FAQView.tsx`. The file content matches expectations and I will apply this change. This will enable the content to scroll. I am confident that the issue of scrollability is solved.


**Evaluating Component State**

I'm now examining how `FAQView` manages its internal state, focusing on `openId`, `activePersonaId`, and the use of `useRef`. My focus is on understanding their effects on component behavior and performance, specifically how changes in `personas`, are handled. I'm reviewing their initialization and updates to ensure everything works correctly. I am confirming data filtering is correctly performed.



**Examining Scroll-to-View Logic**

I'm now diving into the `useEffect` hook and the `scrollIntoView` implementation. My current focus is on understanding the timing of `scrollIntoView` within the `useEffect` to ensure smooth scrolling. I'm investigating how `openId` and `activeItemRef` interact to trigger the scrolling behavior, and I'm ensuring it is consistent and responsive. The smooth scroll functionality is critical to user experience.


**Analyzing Component Logic**

I'm now focused on verifying that the FAQ component renders correctly with content. I'm taking a close look at the provided structure to confirm that the changes have resulted in a functional rendering of FAQs with correct height and scroll behavior, especially for the `<header>` element and the content below. My primary focus is on how it integrates with other components in the parent. I am also making sure the content flows correctly in the scrollable view.


**Verifying Dynamic Content**

I am now closely examining the dynamic content rendering, specifically the `<button>` elements within the `personas.map` loop and the conditional rendering of the `activePersona` section. **Defining Dynamic Categories**

I'm currently focused on making category identification dynamic. Right now, categories are static, hardcoded in `types.ts` using an Enum/Union Type, and in `geminiService.ts`. I need to figure out how to transition from this fixed setup.


**Outlining Data Structures**

I'm now outlining necessary data structure changes. I need to remove the hardcoded `Category` type from `types.ts` and replace it with either a string or a more dynamic approach. I'm also planning to introduce a `CategoryDefinition` type to store metadata, and update `Document` and `FAQItem` accordingly. This will also impact the `GeminiService`, especially the category-related components and methods, and the main app logic needs updating.


**Updating Dynamic Category Handling**

I'm now integrating dynamic category management into the UI. Specifically, I'm updating `FAQView.tsx` to utilize the dynamic category list for grouping instead of the hardcoded list. Additionally, I need to update the admin panel (`AdminPanel.tsx` and `nextcloudService.ts`) to handle category assignment. Initial uploads might default to "General" or require classifying against existing categories, so I am going to have to manage an updated workflow.


**Refining Category Suggestion**

I'm now revising the category suggestion process to enhance stability. I've decided to maintain an `analyzeDocumentCategory` function that leverages the AI to categorize documents. It will use a specified list of existing categories, proposing a new short category name if the document doesn't fit any of them. I'm also planning a `generatedCategories` state. This will be used when generating FAQs, as it allows us to ask the AI to categorize the QA pairs. I will also ask the AI to suggest a category upon document upload. To achieve this, I will change the `Category` type to `string`, and add  `determineDocumentCategory(docContent, existingCategories)` and `generateFAQs` to `geminiService`.


**Implementing Dynamic Category Logic**

I'm now focusing on implementing the dynamic category system in a step-by-step approach. I will change the `Category` type to a string and introduce `INITIAL_CATEGORIES` in `types.ts`. I'll update `geminiService.ts` with a `suggestCategory` function, and modify `generateFAQs` to use dynamic categories. In `App.tsx`, I'll use a `categories` state. The `handleAddDocument` function will be updated to first categorize the document using AI, add new suggested categories to the state, and then generate FAQs.


**Refactoring Admin Panel**

I've refactored the admin panel and related services to improve dynamic category handling. I've removed the hardcoded regex-based category determination from `AdminPanel.tsx` and `nextcloudService.ts`. Instead, `AdminPanel` now just handles uploads, deferring category assignment to `handleAddDocument` in `App.tsx` via AI. This approach keeps the UI snappy while ensuring dynamic categorization. I've also updated `FAQView.tsx` to accept and iterate over a `categories` prop, thus using dynamic category list. `handleExportCSV` is also handled natively with string support.


**Integrating AI Classification**

I'm now integrating AI classification directly into the application flow. Since `geminiService` is frontend-centric in this React setup, I'll leverage it in `App.tsx` after fetching from Nextcloud. I'm simplifying `nextcloudService`, defaulting to "General" for now, and will remove the regex logic, relying on text processing.

I am drafting the code, starting with the Gemini prompt: "Analyze this text. Classify it into one of these existing categories: [list]. If it fits none, create a new concise category name (max 2 words)."
I am planning on making these code changes: `types.ts`: Relax `Category` to `string`.
`services/geminiService.ts`: Add `detectCategory`. Update `generateFAQs`.
`App.tsx`: Manage `categories` state. Integrate `detectCategory` in `handleAddDocument`.
`components/FAQView.tsx`: Render dynamic categories.


**Implementing Category Sorting**

I'm now focusing on category sorting within `FAQView.tsx`. Since dynamic categories lack inherent order, I've decided to sort them alphabetically for improved user experience. Additionally, I'm considering the edge case of an empty category list and plan to initialize with a default category like "General" to prevent empty states. I'll need to revisit the handling of the `geminiService` and `App.tsx` logic to ensure dynamic categories are handled robustly. Specifically, the synchronization between Nextcloud and the dynamic categories needs careful consideration, and I will be simplifying the implementation. I'm focusing on category detection upon document addition, whether manual or from Nextcloud.



**Defining Recategorization Process**

I'm now focusing on a "Recategorize All" functionality for initial setup or migration scenarios, triggered by a button within the UI. I'll need to implement a process in `App.tsx` that iterates through existing documents, dynamically determines their categories, and updates the global category list. I will also make sure this happens when a document is added. I will need to integrate a check that ensures categories are set.


**Finalizing Recategorization Logic**

I'm finalizing the recategorization workflow. I'll add `recalculateCategories` in `App.tsx`, invoking it if the category list is empty but documents exist, to handle the initial setup. I'm focusing on the integration of this logic and how the changes in Nextcloud sync with my dynamic category system.

To make the category identification dynamic, I will remove the hardcoded enums and lists. Instead, the application will maintain a dynamic list of categories in its state (and local storage). When a new document is added, the AI will analyze it to suggest a category—either picking from the existing list or creating a new, concise one if it doesn't fit.

Here are the changes:

1.  **`types.ts`**: Change `Category` from a strict Enum to `string`.
2.  **`services/geminiService.ts`**: Remove hardcoded categories. Add `detectCategory` to analyze documents and update `generateFAQs` to respect the dynamic category list.
3.  **`App.tsx`**: Add state for `categories`. Update `handleAddDocument` to perform AI category detection before saving the document and generating FAQs.
4.  **`components/FAQView.tsx`**: Accept `categories` as a prop and render the view based on this dynamic list instead of the fixed constant.
5.  **`components/AdminPanel.tsx`** & **`services/nextcloudService.ts`**: Remove the regex-based "guesswork" logic for categories, relying instead on the central AI logic in `App.tsx`.
